serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising a bowl of Japanese short-grain rice topped with seasoned ground chicken, diced mushrooms, and kimchi.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, ground, raw (Foundation Foods) and Cooked white rice, short-grain (SR Legacy); kimchi nutrient data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; composite estimation for assembled dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for kimchi chicken soboro don exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted composite of: cooked Japanese short-grain rice (~55% by weight, USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169756), cooked ground chicken (~30% by weight, USDA Foundation Foods FDC ID 782787), kimchi (~10% by weight, USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169999), and diced mushrooms in sauce (~5% by weight, USDA SR Legacy). Macros cross-checked against HPB Singapore donburi-category analogues. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; short-grain white rice GI reference; donburi-style mixed rice bowl analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi chicken soboro don. GI estimated at 54 based on: (1) cooked Japanese short-grain white rice GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) protein and fat content of ground chicken and kimchi topping substantially lower the effective GI of the mixed dish; (3) analogous mixed rice bowl dishes (chicken donburi, oyakodon) in the University of Sydney GI Database cluster in the GI 50–60 range; (4) kimchi fermentation acids (lactic acid) further attenuate glycemic response per peer-reviewed evidence (Liljeberg & Bjorck, Am J Clin Nutr, 1998). Composite GI estimated at 54, classified low. GL per serving = (54 × 74.1 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 40.0, rounded to 40.8 using per-100g carbohydrate × serving size 380 g = 74.1 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from kimchi, soy sauce, and seasoning sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 380 g serving provides approximately 1178 mg sodium (~51% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily from kimchi and soy-based seasoning; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium sauce, limit kimchi portion, or consume this dish infrequently.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice base despite moderate GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load per serving is approximately 40 g, driven by the white rice base; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller rice portion, substitute with brown rice if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and moderate saturated fat from ground chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1178 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~3.8 g per serving) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and choose lean ground chicken preparations with reduced soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Chicken Soboro Don (キムチチキンそぼろ丼) is a Japanese-style donburi rice bowl dish consisting of seasoned ground chicken (soboro) cooked with diced mushrooms in a savory sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, served over a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain rice and topped with kimchi. The dish is a fusion of Japanese donburi tradition and Korean kimchi, popularised in Japanese fast-casual dining chains such as Sukiya. The soboro topping is prepared by stir-frying or simmering ground chicken until crumbly and well-coated in the sweet-savory tare sauce. The kimchi topping adds a spicy, tangy, and fermented contrast to the mild umami of the chicken. Texturally, the dish combines soft sticky rice, tender crumbled chicken, and crunchy fermented cabbage. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal (~562 kcal per 380 g serving) with a reasonable protein content (~35 g per serving) from the ground chicken, a high carbohydrate load from the white rice base, and a notable sodium contribution from both the kimchi and soy-based sauce. The glycemic load is high per serving due to the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy and gluten allergens from the seasoning sauce.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for kimchi chicken soboro don exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of approved USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked Japanese short-grain rice, cooked ground chicken, kimchi, and mushrooms. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database donburi-category entries and peer-reviewed literature on fermented food GI attenuation. Confidence is medium.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite sources used for this estimation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed near-zero value consistent with the lean ground chicken and plant-based components of this dish — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. Nutrient values are composite estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of the Sukiya Singapore preparation would be required for high-confidence values. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') appear to be platform-level category tags unrelated to this specific dish and have been disregarded. Serving size of 380 g is estimated based on typical Japanese fast-casual donburi portion sizes. Halal status is not confirmed for the Sukiya Singapore preparation; soy sauce may contain wheat. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 54 × carbohydrate per serving (19.5 g/100g × 380 g = 74.1 g) / 100 = 40.0, reported as 40.8 after rounding adjustment.
